# Automated Issue Triage — Operator Guide

This repository runs an AI-augmented issue-triage bot on every new and
reopened issue (and, optionally, on the existing backlog). This file is
the **operator runbook**: what's running, how to control it, and how to
turn it off.

## What it does

On every issue that is opened, reopened, or manually dispatched:

1. A deterministic setup step measures issue **age**, **last activity**,
   **comment count**, and whether the **team has ever replied**.
2. The Copilot agent reads the full issue thread and:
   - classifies it (`bug` / `feature request` / `discussion` / `documentation`),
   - suggests topic labels (`cli`, `powershell`, `delete`, `deny settings`,
     `resource provider issue`) where they apply,
   - flags possible duplicates,
   - identifies whether the issue actually belongs in another repo
     (`Azure/bicep`, `Azure/azure-cli`, etc.),
   - drafts a repro plan for bugs,
   - lists what's missing (correlation ID, versions, repro template),
   - links to up to 3 directly-relevant `learn.microsoft.com` pages,
   - posts **one** clearly-marked AI summary comment.
3. It applies labels via `safe-outputs.add-labels` (allowlist below).
4. It **never closes**, **never reopens**, **never @-mentions**, **never
   assigns**, and **never replies twice** on the same run.

Every comment opens with a visible disclaimer that the content is AI-generated and not human-verified.

## Files

| Path | What it is |
|---|---|
| `.github/workflows/issue-triage.md` | Authored skill file (the prompt + frontmatter). **Edit this.** |
| `.github/workflows/issue-triage.lock.yml` | Compiled GitHub Actions workflow. Regenerated by `gh aw compile`. **Do not hand-edit.** |
| `.github/workflows/issue-triage-backlog.yml` | Plain workflow that drains the backlog by dispatching triage runs against existing open issues. |
| `.github/workflows/copilot-setup-steps.yml` | Boilerplate Copilot Coding Agent setup (installs `gh-aw`). |
| `.github/agents/agentic-workflows.agent.md` | Dev-time helper agent for editing gh-aw workflows in VS Code. Not used at runtime. |
| `.github/mcp.json` | MCP server config — wires `gh-aw mcp-server` into Copilot. |
| `.github/aw/actions-lock.json` | SHA-pinned versions of the GitHub Actions the lock file references. |

## Required repository secret

`COPILOT_GITHUB_TOKEN` — a **fine-grained PAT** scoped to this repo with
**Copilot Requests: Read** (Account permission). No other scopes needed.

```bash
# Create the secret (admin only):
gh secret set COPILOT_GITHUB_TOKEN --repo Azure/deployment-stacks
# (paste the PAT when prompted)
```

Pre-filled token-creation URL:
<https://github.com/settings/personal-access-tokens/new?name=COPILOT_GITHUB_TOKEN&description=Azure%2Fdeployment-stacks+Issue+Triage+%E2%80%94+Copilot+engine+authentication&user_copilot_requests=read>

Without this secret set, every triage run will hard-fail at the
`Validate COPILOT_GITHUB_TOKEN secret` step. Set the secret **before**
merging this workflow into `main`.

## How to manually trigger triage on one issue

```bash
gh workflow run issue-triage.lock.yml \
  -F issue_number=123 \
  --repo Azure/deployment-stacks
```

Or, as a maintainer in the GitHub UI: react to the issue with the **👀
(eyes)** emoji and the workflow fires.

## How to enable / disable the backlog catch-up

The backlog workflow (`issue-triage-backlog.yml`) is **inert by default**.
It checks the repository variable `AGENT_TRIAGE_BACKLOG_ENABLED` and
exits immediately unless it is set to the literal string `true`.

```bash
# Start draining the backlog (5 issues/hour, hourly cron):
gh variable set AGENT_TRIAGE_BACKLOG_ENABLED --body true \
  --repo Azure/deployment-stacks

# Pause it (in-flight dispatched runs continue; no new dispatches):
gh variable set AGENT_TRIAGE_BACKLOG_ENABLED --body false \
  --repo Azure/deployment-stacks
```

To dispatch a one-off larger batch (max 25 per run):

```bash
gh workflow run issue-triage-backlog.yml \
  -F max_issues=25 \
  --repo Azure/deployment-stacks
```

## Kill switches (in order of impact)

| Situation | Action |
|---|---|
| Backlog draining too fast / too many comments at once | `gh variable set AGENT_TRIAGE_BACKLOG_ENABLED --body false` |
| Bot is producing low-quality comments on a specific kind of issue | Edit the prompt in `.github/workflows/issue-triage.md`, re-run `gh aw compile`, commit the new `.lock.yml`. |
| Disable triage entirely for newly-opened issues but keep manual dispatch | Comment out the `on.issues:` block in `issue-triage.md` and recompile. |
| **Emergency stop everything** | Revoke `COPILOT_GITHUB_TOKEN` or delete the secret. All triage runs fail at the secret-validation step within seconds; no comments are posted. |

## Labels the bot applies

Whitelist (frontmatter `safe-outputs.add-labels.allowed`):

- `agent:ai-triaged` _(new — single marker label this bot introduces)_
- `bug`, `feature request`, `discussion`, `documentation` _(category)_
- `cli`, `powershell`, `delete`, `deny settings`, `resource provider issue` _(topic)_
- `under investigation`, `waiting for response`, `needs upvote` _(next-step)_

Labels the bot **must not** touch (managed by the team or `fabricbot`):
`committed`, `deploying fix`, `needs triage`, `Needs: Author Feedback`,
`Status: No Recent Activity`.

## Editing the prompt

```bash
# 1. Edit the skill file
$EDITOR .github/workflows/issue-triage.md

# 2. Recompile to regenerate the .lock.yml
gh aw compile

# 3. Commit both files together
git add .github/workflows/issue-triage.md .github/workflows/issue-triage.lock.yml
git commit -m "<what changed>"
git push
```

## Cost / runner usage

Each triage run takes ~2-3 minutes of `ubuntu-latest` runner time
(container startup, AWF firewall, agent execution, safe-outputs upload).
At 5 issues/hour the backlog drains a ~200-issue queue in ~40 hours of
wall time while using ~10 minutes of runner time per hour.

For comparison: the auto-trigger on new issues fires only when an issue
is opened or reopened — typically a handful per day.

## Where the prompt was validated

The skill file was developed and end-to-end tested in a private mirror
repo against 10 representative open issues drawn from this repository.
All 10 runs succeeded; comments were reviewed by a team member before
this PR was opened.
